coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived carbon dots regulating nickel cobalt layered double hydroxide from 2D nanosheets to 3D flower-like spheres as electrodes for enhanced asymmetric supercapacitors

coredata.dc:description: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) often require the use of carbon materials to improve their stability, conductivity, and specific surface area to accommodate new directions in the development of high-performance energy storage materials. Herein, 2D nickel cobalt layered double hydroxide (NCLDH) nanosheets are regulated to form 3D flower-like spheres by fungus bran-derived carbon dots (CDs) via an in situ growth method. The prepared sample (CDs/NCLDH) shows abundant accessible active sites and favorable electrical conductivity, which is aided by strong interactions between CDs and NCLDH. The optimized CDs/NCLDH exhibits significantly enhanced electrochemical performances, including ultrahigh specific capacitance (2100F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and a great rate capability, which are two times higher than those of the NCLDH electrode. Additionally, the asymmetric supercapacitor device assembled with the CDs/NCLDH positive electrode and the fungus bran-derived activated carbon (FBC) negative electrode achieves a superior energy density of 52.5 Wh kg−1 at an ultrahigh powder density of 750 W kg−1. With their simple synthesis method and excellent electrochemical performance, the role of the CDs provides new insights for the development of LDHs with improved performance.

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived carbon dots regulating nickel cobalt layered double hydroxide from 2D nanosheets to 3D flower-like spheres as electrodes for enhanced asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biobased Material Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biobased Material Science and Technology
College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biobased Material Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) often require the use of carbon materials to improve their stability, conductivity, and specific surface area to accommodate new directions in the development of high-performance energy storage materials. Herein, 2D nickel cobalt layered double hydroxide (NCLDH) nanosheets are regulated to form 3D flower-like spheres by fungus bran-derived carbon dots (CDs) via an in situ growth method. The prepared sample (CDs/NCLDH) shows abundant accessible active sites and favorable electrical conductivity, which is aided by strong interactions between CDs and NCLDH. The optimized CDs/NCLDH exhibits significantly enhanced electrochemical performances, including ultrahigh specific capacitance (2100F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and a great rate capability, which are two times higher than those of the NCLDH electrode. Additionally, the asymmetric supercapacitor device assembled with the CDs/NCLDH positive electrode and the fungus bran-derived activated carbon (FBC) negative electrode achieves a superior energy density of 52.5 Wh kg−1 at an ultrahigh powder density of 750 W kg−1. With their simple synthesis method and excellent electrochemical performance, the role of the CDs provides new insights for the development of LDHs with improved performance.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the world has been transitioning away from the use of fossil fuels, and since the beginning of the energy crisis and environmental degradation, the development of sustainable and ecologically sound energy storage devices has become imperative[1,2]. Supercapacitors combine the advantages of conventional capacitors and batteries with the qualities of high power density and good cycling performance[3–5]. Transition metal layer double hydroxides (LDHs) exhibit excellent electrochemical properties due to their superior anion insertion, high capacitance and exchange capacitance[6]. However, the 2D nanosheet structure of LDH materials is prone to agglomerative curling, which limits the availability of active sites during electrochemical testing. Simultaneously, their inherent poor electrical conductivity will inevitably lead to a lower rate performance.

In the construction of electrode materials with heterogeneous structures, carbon materials with different dimensional structures can be combined with LDH materials for the purpose of improving stability and electrical conductivity. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) can be used as substrates to improve the electrical conductivity of NiCo LDH nanosheets due to their unique one-dimensional nanostructure and excellent long-range electrical conductivity[7]. Additionally, Ma et al. used Chingma Abutilon seeds (CAS) as precursors to prepare three-dimensional honeycomb-like porous carbon, which was used to support the flower-like NiMn LDH microspheres. The high conductivity and large specific surface area of the CAS-derived carbon skeleton suggested that it could be selected as the conductive substrate[8].

As an emerging zero-dimensional carbon material, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention in various fields, such as photocatalysis, bioimaging and energy storage devices[9–11]. Existing studies show that composites involving CDs have great potential as excellent electrode materials. CDs can form an effective interface between an electrolyte and an electrode, thus improving the specific capacitance and cycling stability of the electrode[12,13]. CDs increase the ion transfer pathway and volume change gap, and heteroelement-doped CDs carry more organic groups for surface wettability, thus forming a larger interfacial area for contact with the electrolyte, which accelerates the electrochemical reaction[14]. However, when CDs are compounded with other materials, their active sites may become covered due to the aggregation-prone nature of CDs, resulting in degraded performance. Therefore, the introduction of CDs into the gap of NiCo LDH particles should facilitate the uniform dispersion of CDs and the generation of more effective electroactive sites. The preparation of CDs from biomass enables not only the green utilization of natural resources but also the recycling of agricultural and forestry wastes[15,16]. Fungus bran, as one of the widely available wastes in northern China, has a high carbon content and abundant groups, making it an ideal precursor for the preparation of carbon materials with self-doped heteroatoms[17,18]. Therefore, the preparation of CDs based on abundant and renewable fungus bran has wide development prospects and complies with society's need for sustainable development.

Herein, we rationally design and prepare N-doped CDs-regulated NiCo LDH electrode material (CDs/NCLDH), which comprise CDs uniformly distributed on 2D NCLDH nanosheets and regulated to form 3D flower-like spherical structures. Based on NCLDH, regulation by CDs provides a large number of active sites for the diffusion of ions on the surface and inside the native phase, optimizing the ion transport pathway and improving electrical conductivity. Benefiting from the flower-like spheres and the chemically coupled heterogeneous structure of CDs/NCLDH, the interconnected porous network structure delivers a large space to accommodate the volume expansion of the material, which greatly improves the capacitive performance and cycle life of the electrodes. The maximum specific capacitance reached 2100F g−1 at 1 A g−1. To construct an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC), a fungus bran-derived carbon (FBC) negative electrode identical to the CD precursor was synthesized, and its hierarchical porous structure also ensured the excellent specific capacitance performance of the electrode. CDs/NCLDH-3//FBC ASC devices with an output voltage of 1.6 V were constructed in a 3 M KOH electrolyte, which had a high energy density of 52.5 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750 W kg−1, and their cycling stability was maintained after 5000 cycles at 80%.

Nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) and cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O) were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China., hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) and methyl alcohol were procured from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. Fungus bran was collected from our lab and used as the precursor for CDs and FBC. Lysine was supplied by Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. All chemicals were analytically pure and purchased without any further purification.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on an Apreo S HiVac (Thermo Scientific, USA), and energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) element mapping was characterized by an Oxford x-max (ZEISS MERLIN Compact, Germany). Before conducting SEM and EDX characterizations, the surfaces of the samples were coated with a thin layer of gold using an ion sputtering coater to make them conduct electricity. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was observed on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted on an XRD-6100 (Shimadzu, Japan) with graphite-monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA) at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (iS10, Nicolet, USA). A zeta potential tester (Nano ZS90, US) was used to characterize the zeta potential. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted with Al X-rays at 140 W (THERMO, USA) to characterize the chemical state of the samples. Raman spectra were recorded using an LabRAM HR Evolution (HORIBA Scientific, France). The specific surface area and pore size distributions were obtained using a Brunauer–Emmett–Teller instrument (BET) (Autosorb-IQ-MP, Quantachrome, USA) via the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method.

Fungus brans were washed, dried and ground into powder. Fungus bran (0.75 g) and lysine (0.125 g) were added to 30 mL of deionized water and stirred at room temperature until well mixed. The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the upper layer of the solution was centrifuged, and clarified liquid was collected. The clarified solution was filtered and stored at 4 °C.

For the synthesis of CDs/NCLDHs, 4 mmol NiCl2·6H2O, 8 mmol CoCl2·6H2O, a certain volume of CDs solution and 0.125 g of HMTA were added into 30 mL of methyl alcohol and then stirred to complete dissolution. A Teflon vessel (autoclave) filled with the resulting solution was placed into a stainless steel shell. Then, the autoclave was sealed and kept at 120 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the products were completely washed using deionized water and ethanol, and green powders were obtained after drying at 60 °C for 6 h. The samples with different CD contents (1, 2, 3, and 4 wt%) were named CDs/NCLDH-x (x = 1, 2, 3, and 4 wt%, respectively).

FBC was prepared by a one-step carbonation activation method. In the first step, 2 g of fungus bran was dipped in an aqueous solution containing 2 g of potassium permanganate and heated at 90 °C for 1 h. The reaction was heated to 700 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature. After natural cooling, the solid was soaked in H2SO4 solution (0.5 M) overnight, and FBC was obtained following vacuum filtration, washing, and vacuum drying (60 °C, 12 h).

The electrochemical performance of the samples was identified using standard procedures[19]. The slurry for preparing the electrode material was obtained by mixing activated carbon, carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene emulsion (PTFE) in ethanol in a mass ratio of 8:1:1. The slurry was then applied and pressed onto nickel foam (1 cm × 1 cm) and dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h. The mass loading of the electrode material was approximately 3 mg cm−2. In a conventional three-electrode system, 3 M KOH was used as the electrolyte for electrochemical testing. Pt slices and saturated calomel electrodes (SCEs) were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. An ASC device was assembled by means of 3 M KOH electrolyte that uses CDs/NCLDH-3 and FBC electrodes. All electrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The specific capacitance (C, F g−1) of the electrodes is calculated from the charge/discharge curve by the following equations[20]:
(1)
C =
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
where I is the current density, ΔV is the potential window, Δt is the discharge time, and m is the mass of the active material.

According to the charge balance theory, the mass ratio of positive and negative electrodes needed to ensure the best performance of the ASC device is calculated by the equation[21]:
(2)

where m+ and m- are the masses of the positive and negative electrodes, respectively; C+ and C- are the specific capacitances; and V+ and V- are the potential windows of the positive and negative electrodes, respectively.

The energy density (E) and power density (P) were calculated by the following equations[22]:

Fig. 1 shows details of the preparation of CDs/NCLDHs. The green and renewable biomass waste, fungus bran, can be used not only as a precursor for CDs but also to prepare activated carbon materials by the carbonization activation method. NCLDH was first prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, and then the CDs were added to synthesize CDs/NCLDH composites, as described in the experimental section. First, CDs enhance the electrical conductivity of the composites and accelerate the surface charge transfer. Second, the regulating effect of CDs on composites is attributed to the adsorption of negatively charged CDs onto metal ions, which controls crystal growth. Finally, the surface of CDs is rich in functional groups, which can stabilize surface state and improve specific capacitance and stability. Based on the above considerations, the anchoring of CDs into NCLDH is an effective strategy to prepare composites with excellent electrochemical properties.

Fig. S1 shows the SEM and TEM morphologies of the NCLDH. As shown in Fig. S1a, the NCLDH are composed of curved nanosheets that are interconnected with each other, and the Ni, Co and O elements are uniformly distributed across the nanosheets, as revealed by EDX mapping (Fig. S1b-1e). TEM images confirmed significant nanosheet structures, and the thickness of the NCLDH nanosheets was approximately 6 nm. A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image showed lattice spacings of 0.256 nm and 0.37 nm with a good match to the (012) and (006) planes of the NCLDH (Fig. S1f-1 g)[23,24].

The structures of CDs/NCLDH with various contents of CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The surface morphology of CDs/NCLDH-1 is shown in Fig. 2a. When the CD content is 1 wt%, a flower-like spherical morphology is formed, but the size is not uniform, and the nanosheets are still connected in a large area. As shown in Fig. 2b, when the content of CDs increased to 2 wt%, independent flower-like spheres with a diameter of 1.6 μm appeared in the SEM images of sample CDs/NCLDH-2. Unfortunately, some of the flower-like spheres still had morphological defects, and others were only half formed, thus appearing as cross-sectional structures. SEM images of CDs/NCLDH-3 in Fig. 2c clearly demonstrate that the composites effectively inherit the nanosheet arrays of NCLDH and form flower-like spheres with a diameter of 2 μm. The reason for the apparent morphological changes is because of the coordination effect of zero-dimensional CDs, which can deposit on the nanosheets’ surface and prevent the nanosheets from assembling haphazardly, resulting in regular spherical structures[25]. Additionally, the larger diameter of the flower-like sphere is due to the incorporation of CDs, as the large amount of oxygen negative ions in CDs can attract more metal ions to form metal-oxygen bonds to make the nanosheets larger in size. CDs/NCLDHs are assembled from nanosheets into a 3D spherical structure. This special flower-like spherical structure provides sufficient space for charge transfer and ion penetration and is considered to be a potential material for improving electrochemical performance[26]. When the concentration of CDs is further increased, the attraction of oxygen negative ions to metals in the CDs is greater than its restricting effect on nanosheet assembly, and in Fig. 2d, the size of the nanosheets comprising CDs/NCLDH-4 became significantly larger and thicker, losing their flower-like spherical morphology. These phenomena suggest that carbon dots play an important role in the control of NCLDH morphology because carbon dots are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups[27].

The size range of CDs is within the range of 3.5 to 5.5 nm. (Fig. S2). The HRTEM image (inset in the lower left corner of Fig. S2b) shows that the lattice spacing is 0.28 nm. Fig. 2e-2f show that the homogeneous bonding of CDs with particles of NCLDH forms a flower-like structure, and that the interior of the spheres is also composed of nanoparticles. This demonstrates that CDs are mainly distributed on the surface of the composites and fill in gaps between NCLDH nanoparticles, and that this structure makes CDs fully accessible to the electrolyte, while the functional groups on them generate pseudocapacitance, which can promote rapid carrier transfer during charging and discharging. The HRTEM image shows that CDs/NCLDH-3 retains the (006) and (012) crystal planes, and lattice fringes corresponding to the graphitic carbon (020) plane with a size of 0.28 nm appear in the image[28]. To verify the distribution of each element in CDs/NCLDH-3, element mapping analysis was performed. Since carbon film conductive adhesive was used for the test, the C element was not tested to ensure the accuracy of the test. As shown in Fig. 2g-2 k, the Ni, Co, O and N elements are uniformly distributed in the composites. The N elements are derived from CDs, which further confirms that CDs have been successfully attached to the NCLDH surface. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern can be observed in Fig. 2l, where the clearly diffracted spots and diffraction rings reveal the ordered structure of CDs/NCLDH-3[29].

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of NCLDH and CDs/NCLDHs, where the characteristic diffraction peaks are consistent with the standard JCPDS data of the NCLDH structure. Most sharp XRD peaks of NCLDH at 11.7°, 23.0°, 34.6° and 60.2° correspond to the (003), (006), (012) and (110) planes [30], which agrees with HRTEM analysis. Due to the low content and crystallinity of CDs in the composites, the diffraction peaks of the CD/NCLDHs barely changed when the composites were coupled with CDs, indicating that the effect of CDs on the crystallinity and structure of NCLDH can be neglected. It is worth noting that the diffraction peaks of CDs/NCLDHs are enhanced compared to those of NCLDH in the range of 20-25°, which is caused by the successful incorporation of CDs and NCLDH seen in the EDX mapping results[31].

The FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 3b were used to characterize the surface functional groups of CDs, NCLDH and CDs/NCLDHs composites. A broad peak located at 3335 cm−1 is responsible for the NH group[32]. The absorption bands at 2933 and 2867 cm−1 represent the CH stretching vibrations, while the bands at 1581 and 1626 cm−1 are attributed to CN and CO[15]. The characteristic peaks appearing at 1763, 1453, 1158 and 1050 cm−1 are attributed to CO, CN, COC and CO, respectively[16]. The stretching vibration peaks of CDs/NCLDH composites in the range of 500–1000 cm−1 are caused by the lattice of metal-oxygen and metal-oxygen-metal bonds on the laminate, which supports the conjecture in the SEM characterization analysis[33]. Furthermore, compared with NCLDH, the vibration peaks shifted and widened. In summary, the as-synthesized CDs/NCLDH composites simultaneously showed characteristic absorption peaks of both NCLDH and CDs. Fig. 3c displays the Raman spectra of NCLDH and CDs/NCLDH-3, and the characteristic peaks (Metal-Oxygen-Metal bonds) at 450 and 524 cm−1 belong to NCLDH[34]. The two diffraction peaks of CDs at 1343 and 1542 cm−1 correspond to the D and G bands of structural defects and graphitization, respectively[35]. CDs/NCLDH-3 contained all four characteristic peaks simultaneously, indicating the successful synthesis of the sample. The CDs have a zeta potential of −5.4 mV in water (Fig. 3d), and the metal oxides have a positive charge that interacts well with the negatively charged CDs. Therefore, CDs can be used as core sites for the growth of NCLDH nanosheets to form CDs/NCLDH-3 flower-like sphere composites[36,37].

The surface area and porous properties of the samples were tested by the nitrogen adsorption and desorption results shown in Fig. 4 a-4b, respectively[38]. The observed type IV isotherms with an H3-type hysteresis loop suggest that the samples have a mesoporous structure[15]. The specific area of CDs/NCLDH-3 (180 m2 g−1) is approximately twice that of NCLDH (80 m2 g−1), indicating that the contact area between the electrode and electrolyte can be greatly improved by the CD-regulated heterostructure of the flower-like spheres. The dispersion of CDs into the nanosheets of CDs/NCLDH-3 during in situ growth prevents the nanosheets from accumulating, thus increasing the specific surface area of the CDs/NCLDH materials. However, excess CDs covering the nanosheet surface blocked the transport channels and destroyed the pore structure, resulting in a decrease of the specific surface area of CDs/NCLDH-4 (Table S1)[39]. In addition, the pore size distribution obtained from the desorption portion of the BJH isotherm (Fig. 4b) shows that the range of average pore size is 5–10 nm. Flower-like sphere morphologies with precisely porous structures facilitate improved electrochemical performance due to their open structure and large surface area providing more electroactive sites for chemical reactions, greatly shortening the ion diffusion length and accelerating the ion migration rate.

The five peaks of the total XPS spectrum of CDs/NCLDH-3 were attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, Ni 2p and Co 2p, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Fig. 5b shows the C 1 s spectrum of CDs/NCLDH-3, where the four main peaks can be fitted with four peaks. The peaks at 281.4, 284.6, 285.7 and 288.3 eV are characteristic of C-metal, CC, CO and sp2-hybridized carbon in CO [40,41], indicating that the sample was successfully doped with nitrogen (Fig. 5c)[42]. As shown in Fig. 5d, the peaks at 530.5 eV are the result of metal-oxygen bonding. The peaks attributed to the OH group were observed at 531.4 eV. The peak at 532.7 eV is attributed to absorbed oxygen, and after CD modification, the O 1 s spectrum of CDs/NCLDH-3 shows a more intense peak at 532.7 eV due to the presence of C-OH in CDs[43,44]. Fig. 5e shows the Ni 2p spectra of NCLDH and CDs/NCLDH-3. The two main peaks centered at 855.3 and 872.7 eV correspond to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively. In addition, there are two satellite peaks centered at 861.1 and 879.3 eV. The XPS spectra of Ni 2p3/2 can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 854.9 and 856.2 eV, corresponding to Ni2+ and Ni3+, while the binding energies at 872.7 and 874.6 eV of Ni 2p1/2 are designated as Ni2+ and Ni3+ [45]. Similarly, the Co 2p spectra (Fig. 5f) show two peaks centered at 781 eV and 796.8 eV with their corresponding satellite peaks at 786.1 and 803.2 eV, belonging to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively. In addition, the two fitting peaks of Co 2p located at 782.5 and 797.5 eV can be ascribed to Co2+, while the peaks at 780.8 and 796.4 correspond to Co3+, indicating the presence of both Co2+ and Co3+ [34,46]. This XPS result ensures the existence of trivalent metal ions in NCLDH. It has been reported that the substitution of Ni2+ by trivalent cations can generate a positive charge on the sheets, and the interlayer anions can compensate for this additional charge, thus stabilizing the α-phase hydroxide and improving the electrochemical properties of NCLDH and CDs/NCLDH-3[47].

As shown in Fig. 6 , the electrochemical performance of the prepared NCLDH and CDs/NCLDHs in the three-electrode system was tested. Fig. 6a shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 in a voltage window of 0–0.6 V. The electrodes show obvious redox peaks, and the corresponding capacitances mainly originate from the reversible reactions of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+. The equations are as follows[48]:
(5) Ni(OH)2 + OH–→NiOOH + H2O + e-
(6) Co(OH)2 + OH–→CoOOH + H2O + e-
(7) CoOOH + OH–→CoO2 + H2O + e-

Compared with that of NCLDH, the current density response of CDs/NCLDHs electrodes was larger for the same scan rate, indicating a higher capacitance than that of the NCLDH electrode. The results show that CDs can effectively increase the capacitance of NCLDH, with the highest capacitance value of CDs/NCLDH-3. As shown in Fig. 6b, the CV curves of CDs/NCLDH-3 show good and stable capacitance performance at different scan rates. Fig. 6c shows the GCD curves for NCLDH and CDs/NCLDHs at a current density of 1 A g−1 in the 0–0.5 V potential window. Therefore, CDs/NCLDH-3 was further investigated.

Fig. 6d shows the GCD curves of CDs/NCLDH-3 at different current densities. The capacitance values of CDs/NCLDH-3 were 2100, 1996, 1896, 1660, 1488 and 1300 F g−1 at current densities of 1, 2, 3, 5, 8 and 10 A g−1, respectively, which were higher than those of other composite samples (Fig. S3). The specific capacitance of the samples was calculated from the discharge curves (Fig. 6e). The calculated specific capacitances of the NCLDH, CDs/NCLDH-1, CDs/NCLDH-2, CDs/NCLDH-3 and CDs/NCLDH-4 electrodes at 1 A g−1 were 1020, 1532, 1846, 2100 and 1914 F g−1, respectively. The specific capacitance of CDs/NCLDH-3 was almost twice that of NCLDH and higher than other results reported in the literatures (Table 1 ). When the current density was increased to 10 A g−1, the specific capacitances of the five electrodes became 336, 1109, 1220, 1300 and 1140 F g−1. The capacitance retention of CDs/NCLDH-3 was 61.9%. The specific capacitance of the composites gradually decreases with increasing current density, which means that the adsorption of electrolyte ions on the electrode decreases during accelerated charging and discharging. Nevertheless, the CDs/NCLDH-3 electrode still exhibited good overall rate performance. The specific capacitance of the CDs/NCLDH materials all improved to a greater degree after the addition of CDs, probably due to the higher conductivity, surface wettability and charge transfer efficiency of CDs, which can effectively increase the specific capacitance of the samples[49]. However, the use of excessive CDs lead to a decrease in specific capacitance due to reduced NCLDH content. Therefore, an appropriate amount of CDs addition is essential to optimize the specific capacitance of CDs/NCLDHs electrodes[50].

The reaction kinetics of the resulting samples were further explored using CV curves. Notably, their storage mechanisms were estimated qualitatively by the relationship between i and ν, as shown by equation[58]:
(8)
i
=
av
b
where a is a constant and b is obtained from the slope value of the log(i)-log(ν) plot, as displayed in Fig. 6f. It is well known that a b value of 0.5 indicates the diffusion control process, while a b value of 1.0 indicates the capacitance control behavior. In general, the capacitance control process consists of ion adsorption/desorption and surface redox reactions for CDs/NCLDH-3, which have b values of 0.53 and 0.61, respectively, indicating the mixed contribution of diffusion-controlled and capacitance-controlled processes. Thus, the electrode has battery-like and supercapacitor-like characteristics. The percentage of capacitive-controlled contribution is quantified by equation[59]:
(9)
i ( V ) = k
1
v + k
2
v
1 / 2
where i(V) is the total current at the appropriate fixed potential, and k1v and k2v1/2 are the contributions of the capacitive and diffusive control charges, respectively. Additionally, the contribution ratio of the capacitance of CDs/NCLDH-3 increases significantly with increasing scan rate, as shown in Fig. 6g. The contribution ratio was as high as 52% despite a scan rate of only 10 mV s−1, which better clarifies the fast electrochemical kinetics and outstanding multiplicative capability.

Fig. 6h shows the Nyquist curves of different samples. The CDs/NCLDH-3 electrode shows the smallest semicircle in the high frequency region with the smallest charge transfer resistance (Rct) compared to that of the NCLDH and other CDs/NCLDHs electrodes. The fitted results show that the charge transfer resistance of CDs/NCLDH-3 is only 0.72 Ω (the equivalent circuit diagram and the fitting data are shown in Fig. S4 and Table S2). Similarly, the curve of the CDs/NCLDH-3 electrode in the low frequency region is closer to the imaginary axis, indicating a lower diffusion resistance inside the material. The appropriate amounts of CDs facilitate the diffusion of electrolyte ions to the electrode surface. The cycle stability of the CDs/NCLDH-3 electrode was examined at a high current density of 20 A g−1 under a potential window of 0–0.5 V (Fig. S5). The CDs/NCLDH-3 electrode showed a capacitance retention of 93.4% and a Coulombic efficiency of 97.4% after 2000 cycles. The inset graphs exhibit the GCD curves for the first 10 cycles and the last 10 cycles. The Rct of CDs/NCLDH-3 before and after 1000 cycles is 0.71 and 0.75 Ω, respectively, and the curves in the low frequency region have relatively similar slopes, suggesting that CDs/NCLDH-3 still has a low resistance and a fast kinetic diffusion rate and that the ion diffusion rate remains almost unchanged after 1000 cycles[60]. These results indicate that the oxygen-containing functional groups derived from CDs act as a bridge for charge transfer, which helps to improve the utilization of NCLDH and suppress electrochemical polarization. The regular spherical structure greatly increases the specific surface area, resulting in assembled supercapacitors with higher rate capability and coulombic efficiency[61].

To further evaluate the electrochemical performance of the CDs/NCLDH-3 electrode in practical applications, CDs/NCLDH-3 and FBC were used as positive and negative electrodes, respectively, and the assembled ASC is shown in Fig. 7 a. The mass ratio of the positive and negative electrodes was estimated according to charge balance theory. In Fig. 7b, the CV curves of the positive and negative electrodes were tested at different potential windows at 10 mV s−1. The electrochemical performance of the FBC is shown in Fig. S6. The strong correlation between CDs/NCLDH-3 and FBC is necessary for their assembly into high-performance storage devices. Fig. S7a shows the CV profiles of the ASC device registered at different scan rates for the 0–1.6 V voltage window. The CV profiles do not change at different scan rates between 10 mV s−1 and 100 mV s−1, showing the good rate performance of the prepared ASC device. The specific capacitances of ASC devices are calculated from the GCD curves (Fig. 7c), as shown in Fig. 7d, and are calculated as 166.7 ± 3.2, 124.0 ± 4.0, 113.3 ± 4.2, 90.7 ± 3.1, 76.3 ± 4.5, 68.0 ± 3.6, and 54.7 ± 7.2 F g−1 at 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, and 10 A g−1, respectively. At a current density of 5 times the initial value, the specific capacitance remains at 45% of the initial specific capacitance, and when the current density is increased to 10 times the initial value, the specific capacitance remains at 32%, indicating that the ASC device has good stability at higher current densities. Fig. 7e reveals a long-term cycling test at a current density of 20 A g−1. More notably, after 5000 cycles, the ASC device maintains 80% of its initial capacitance, indicating a moderate cycling performance in practical applications. The specific capacitance of the ASC device increases after 2000 cycles, which is caused by an increase in magnetic permeability due to ion insertion/desertion during charge/discharge and the activation of the electrode materials[62,63]. The excellent electrochemical performance of the CDs/NCLDH-3//FBC ASC device was attributed to the rational integration of electrode materials and structural design. The synergistic effect of CDs and NCLDH plays a crucial role in the improvement of device performance.

As shown in Fig. 7f, the Ragone plots between the energy density and power density of the CDs/NCLDH-3//FBC ASC device are calculated based on the total quality of both the positive and negative electrodes, demonstrating a high energy density of 52.5 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750 W kg−1. These values are comparable to those of previous literature reporting LDH-related materials, including NiCo LDH/IPC//IPC (29.4 Wh kg−1, 744 W kg−1)[35], NiCo-LDH@PANI@CC//AC (8.4 Wh kg−1 6200 W kg−1) [64], CC/NiCoP@NiCo-LDH//AC (57 Wh kg−1, 850 W kg−1) [65], C-LDH/Co9S8//AC (47.3 Wh kg−1, 1505 W kg−1) [66], CMF@NiAl-LDH//PC (45.2 Wh kg−1, 170 W kg−1) [67], NCO@NiCo-LDH//(NGH) (50.7 Wh kg−1, 773 W kg−1)[68], and AC//LDH-RGO-CNFs (33.7 Wh kg−1, 785.8 W kg−1)[69]. As shown in Fig. S7b, two assembled CDs/NCLDH-3//FBC ASC devices can light up a 3 V LED after 60 s of series charging, which demonstrates a promising energy storage device for application.

In summary, this study set out to design an in situ growth method to synthesize CDs/NCLDH nanocomposites and regulate the morphology of NCLDH utilizing CDs to enable the change from 2D nanosheets to 3D flower-like spheres. The results show that the CDs/NCLDH-3 electrode provided a specific capacitance of 2100F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and retained approximately 62% of its specific capacitance when the current density was increased by a factor of 10. This study provides insight into a means of increasing the capacitance of flower-like structured electrodes by leveraging the synergistic effect between CDs and NCLDH nanosheets. Moreover, assembling ASC delivers maximum energy density (52.5 Wh kg−1) and relative power density (750 W kg−1). Compared with the previously reported NCLDH electrode material[64,70], the synergistic effect between CDs and NCLDH nanosheets accelerates interfacial charge transfer and ion transport, and thus improves the capacitance of the electrodes. A limitation of this study is that only the modulating effect of CDs on NCLDH nanosheets was established, and further work could assess the effect of CDs on the other structures of LDH-based materials and assist in the search for methods to improve cycling stability and capacitive performance.
